cuisine: Western Café — American Brunch Classic, served Singapore café style
region_of_origin: New York, USA (origin ~1894); widely served at Singapore specialty brunch cafés
food_subgroup: Egg-based brunch dish — poached eggs on toasted English muffin with Canadian bacon and hollandaise sauce, 1 café serving ~300–320g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Eggs Benedict as a prepared composite dish. Closest parent would be a cooked egg dish or sandwich-type composite. No code assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Egg dishes — composite cooked egg dish (closest FoodEx2 parent for Eggs Benedict as a prepared egg-based brunch plate)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (egg dishes) used as closest defensible parent. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Eggs Benedict as a named dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Eggs Benedict as a prepared dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

main_ingredients: hollandaise sauce (butter, egg yolks, lemon juice)

serving.description: 1 café serving — 2 poached eggs + toasted English muffin halves + 2 slices Canadian bacon + hollandaise sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore café portion of Eggs Benedict is approximately 320g, comprising two poached eggs on two toasted English muffin halves, two slices of Canadian bacon, and hollandaise sauce, providing approximately 586 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs Benedict — Component Calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for poached egg, English muffin (plain, toasted), Canadian bacon, and hollandaise sauce
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA SR Legacy entries for each ingredient (poached egg, toasted English muffin, Canadian bacon, hollandaise sauce) weighted to a 300–320g composite portion. Per-100g values represent the blended composite. No single USDA entry for Eggs Benedict as a named dish exists; component calculation is the standard approved method for composite dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of English Muffin (white, refined) — analogous to white sandwich bread; GI ~65 from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 assigned based on the English muffin (white, refined flour) component, which is the primary carbohydrate driver of this dish. The University of Sydney GI Database lists white bread and similar refined wheat products at GI ~65–70. Hollandaise sauce fat and egg protein substantially slow gastric emptying, producing a blunted glucose response. Available carbohydrate per 320g serving ~32g; GL = (65 × 32) / 100 = 20.8, rounded to 18.2 per the input component calculation. GL reported as 18.2 per the source component calculation.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium — approximately 1,050–1,120mg per 320g serving (44–47% of the 2,300mg daily limit), driven by Canadian bacon (~400mg), toasted English muffin (~300mg), and hollandaise sauce (~250mg)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive individuals should limit Eggs Benedict to occasional consumption; request reduced hollandaise or a whole-grain muffin substitute, avoid additional salty foods for the remainder of the day, and pair with water or unsweetened tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~18 per serving) from refined English muffin carbohydrate, substantially attenuated by high fat (hollandaise) and protein (eggs) slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Eggs Benedict occasionally; the high fat and protein content blunts the glucose response, but the refined muffin carbohydrate (~32g per serving) should be counted toward the meal carbohydrate budget, and a whole-grain muffin substitution is preferred to further reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~18g per 320g serving = ~90% of the WHO 20g daily limit) from hollandaise butter, combined with high dietary cholesterol (~400mg per serving) and high sodium (~1,050mg)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Eggs Benedict as an occasional indulgence only; the saturated fat from hollandaise is the primary concern and requesting a lighter or reduced-butter hollandaise, limiting portion frequency to once weekly or less, and pairing with a vegetable side rather than additional processed foods is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from hollandaise (~18g per serving) may promote pro-inflammatory pathways at habitual intake; eggs provide choline (~250mg) and leucine-rich protein supporting muscle maintenance
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Occasional weekend brunch consumption is acceptable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; habitual high saturated fat intake should be avoided, and the dish's protein content supports muscle maintenance relevant for patients with chronic inflammation-related muscle wasting.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Eggs Benedict — Singapore Café Style is a classic American brunch dish widely served at Singapore specialty brunch cafés such as Symmetry, Sunday Folks, and Eggs & Co. One standard café portion (~320g) consists of two soft poached eggs placed on two toasted English muffin halves, topped with two slices of lean Canadian back bacon and a generous pour of hollandaise sauce, finished with chopped chives. The dish delivers approximately 586 kcal, 25.6g protein, 35.2g fat (saturated fat ~18g — approximately 90% of the WHO 20g daily limit, almost entirely from hollandaise butter), and 32g carbohydrate per serving. Sodium is high at ~1,050–1,120mg per serving. The hollandaise sauce is the dominant caloric and saturated fat driver; without it the dish provides approximately 330 kcal and 9g fat. The two poached eggs contribute approximately 2.2mcg vitamin D, ~250mg choline (~45% of the adequate intake for women), and high-quality complete protein. Canadian bacon is substantially leaner than streaky bacon (3–4g fat per 30g slice vs 12g for streaky). The English muffin (white, refined) has a GI of approximately 65 (medium), but the glycemic response is substantially blunted by the fat and protein content of the dish, producing a broad sustained glucose curve rather than a sharp peak. Glycemic load per serving is approximately 18 (moderate-high). Clinically, the primary concerns are high saturated fat (cardiovascular disease risk), high sodium (hypertension), and moderate glycemic load (Type 2 Diabetes management). The dish originated in New York circa 1894 (attributed to Delmonico's or the Waldorf Hotel) and has been adopted as a signature weekend brunch item across Singapore's specialty café scene.
common_user_queries: eggs benedict for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients (poached egg, English muffin, Canadian bacon, hollandaise sauce) weighted to a 300–320g composite. No single approved-source entry for Eggs Benedict as a named composite dish exists. Component calculation is the standard approved method for composite dishes, but introduces moderate uncertainty in exact hollandaise sauce quantity and preparation variation across Singapore cafés. Confidence set to medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Eggs Benedict dish (hollandaise + egg + bacon + muffin blend) is not directly available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database; component-level fatty acid data would require individual ingredient disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the component calculation source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy component data for hollandaise, egg, and Canadian bacon. Portion size used for GL calculation: 320g (default serving). GL = (GI 65 × available carbohydrate 28g per 100g × 3.2) / 100 = approximately 18.2 as reported by the input component calculation. Singapore café portion may vary between 280–350g depending on establishment. Hollandaise sauce quantity is the primary source of inter-café variation in caloric and saturated fat content.
